---
title: "Why a Line? The Regression Question"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Regression"
---

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is **REGR-0**, the opener of the
> Regression track. It assumes nothing above the knowledge floor — functions, a
> scatterplot, and the idea of an average. It is the *motivational* article: it
> poses the question the whole track answers and tells the origin story, but it
> does **not** derive the line. The algebra — choosing squared-error loss and
> solving for the slope and intercept — is the job of **REGR-1**, which this
> article sets up.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

We open the Regression track not with a formula but with a question: when we
"fit a line to data," *what are we actually estimating, and why is it a line?*
The single claim this article defends is that regression is the estimation of a
**conditional mean**, $E[Y \mid X]$ — the average value of an outcome given what
we know — and that the familiar straight line is the simplest honest guess at
that function. Along the way we show that "draw the best line through the cloud"
is *ill-posed* until we say what *best* means, which is exactly the choice
REGR-1 makes. We tell the history that forces all of this into existence:
Galton's discovery of *regression to the mean*, and Legendre and Gauss
inventing least squares to pin down planets and the shape of the Earth.

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
**Regression estimates the conditional mean $E[Y \mid X]$ — the average outcome
given the inputs. A "line" is the simplest model of that mean, and "best line"
means nothing until we choose a rule for scoring our errors.**
:::

# The one question

Picture the everyday gesture that names this entire field. You have a cloud of
points — heights of fathers against heights of their sons, square footage
against house price, advertising spend against sales — and you draw a line
through it. Everyone has done it; it feels obvious. But pause on it the way a
sharp interviewer would, because almost every idea in this track is hiding
inside that gesture.

Two questions are buried there, and they are *different* questions.

- **The prediction question.** A new house comes on the market at 2{,}100
  square feet. What price should I guess? Here I do not care *why* the line
  looks the way it does; I want a number, and I want it to be wrong as rarely
  and as mildly as possible on houses I have not seen yet.
- **The explanation question.** Does an extra hundred square feet *raise* the
  price, and by how much, holding everything else fixed? Here the number on the
  line — its slope — is the whole point. I want to interpret it, attach
  uncertainty to it, and defend it.

The same line can serve both, but the two goals pull on it differently, and a
lot of confusion in applied work comes from not knowing which one you are
doing. We will keep both in view through the whole track; for now, notice that
both of them want the *same underlying object*. The predictor wants to know "on
average, what outcome comes with this input?" The explainer wants to know "how
does that average shift as I change the input?" Both are questions about an
**average outcome as a function of the inputs**. That object has a name.

## Regression is the estimation of a conditional mean

Let $Y$ be the outcome we care about (price, son's height, sales) and let $X$
be what we get to condition on (square footage, father's height, ad spend).
For any fixed value $X = x$, there is not *one* outcome — there is a whole
distribution of them. At exactly 2{,}100 square feet, houses sell for many
different prices: location, condition, and luck scatter them. So asking "what
*is* the price at 2{,}100 sq ft?" is the wrong question; there is no single
answer.

The right question is: **what is the average price among houses at 2{,}100 sq
ft?** That average — taken over the distribution of $Y$ at a fixed $x$ — is the
**conditional mean**, written

$$
m(x) \;=\; E[\,Y \mid X = x\,].
$$

Read the notation slowly, because it is the spine of everything. $E[\cdot]$ is
the *expectation* — a weighted average of all possible values, weighted by how
likely each is. (Formally, for a discrete outcome it is $\sum_y y \, P(Y = y)$;
for a continuous one it is $\int y \, f(y)\,dy$. We only need the *idea* of an
average here; the Probability track builds expectation properly, and we use it
seriously starting in REGR-2.) The vertical bar $\mid X = x$ says: take that
average *only over the cases where the input equals $x$*. As $x$ sweeps across
its possible values, $m(x)$ traces out a curve — the **regression function**.

::: {#def-regfn}
**Definition (regression function).** For an outcome $Y$ and predictor $X$, the
*regression function* is the conditional mean
$$
m(x) = E[\,Y \mid X = x\,],
$$
the average value of $Y$ among all cases with $X = x$. *Regression* is the
problem of estimating $m(x)$ from a finite sample of paired observations
$(x_1, y_1), \dots, (x_n, y_n)$.
:::

Why is the conditional mean the *right* target, and not, say, the conditional
median or the most likely value? There is a precise reason, and it is worth
stating now even though we will only prove it later. The conditional mean is the
function of $x$ that makes the **smallest average squared error** when you use
it to predict $Y$. In symbols, among *all possible functions* $g$,

$$
m(x) = E[Y \mid X = x] \quad\text{minimizes}\quad E\big[(Y - g(X))^2\big].
$$

So *if* you have decided that being wrong by 2 is four times as bad as being
wrong by 1 — that is what squaring does — then the conditional mean is, with no
further assumptions, the best possible predictor. That "if" is doing enormous
work, and it is the hinge of the next section. (This minimization fact is the
*population* version of the squared-error story; REGR-1 does the *sample*
version with calculus, and REGR-23 returns to estimate $m(x)$ directly without
assuming a line.)

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"What is regression actually estimating?"** The crisp answer interviewers
want is *the conditional expectation $E[Y \mid X]$* — not "a line," not "the
relationship," but specifically the average of the outcome given the inputs. A
line is one *model* of that mean. Saying this cleanly signals you understand
that the line is a means, not the end, and it makes everything downstream
(loss functions, residuals, why we square errors) follow naturally.
:::

## Why a line, then?

If the target is the curve $m(x)$, why do we so often draw a *straight* line —
the most restrictive curve imaginable? Three honest reasons, in increasing order
of importance.

1. **It is the simplest non-trivial model.** A line $m(x) \approx \beta_0 +
   \beta_1 x$ has exactly two numbers in it: an intercept $\beta_0$ (where it
   crosses) and a slope $\beta_1$ (how fast it climbs). Two numbers is the least
   you can spend and still capture "outcome tends to rise (or fall) with input."

2. **The slope is directly interpretable.** $\beta_1$ is "the average change in
   $Y$ per one-unit change in $X$." That is the explanation question's answer in
   a single number. No curve gives you so clean a story.

3. **With few data points, a flexible curve fools itself.** If you let the model
   bend freely, it will happily thread every point in your sample — including
   the noise — and then predict garbage on new data. A line *cannot* chase
   noise; its rigidity is a feature when data is scarce. This is the
   bias–variance trade-off, and the whole nonparametric branch of the track
   (REGR-20 through REGR-26) is about earning the right to bend the line *only
   as much as the data supports*.

The honest caveat — which we owe you up front, per the spirit of this series —
is that the world is rarely exactly linear. The line is a deliberate
*approximation* to $m(x)$, chosen because it is interpretable and stable, not
because nature is straight. Knowing when that approximation is good enough, and
what to reach for when it is not, is a recurring theme; we flag the boundary
explicitly in the closing section.

```{python}
#| label: fig-conditional-mean
#| fig-cap: "The target of regression is the conditional mean. At each input value there is a whole spread of outcomes (faint points); regression estimates the average outcome at each input (red curve). A straight line (dashed) is a simple model of that mean — here a good one over the middle, straining at the edges."
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(0)

# A gently curved true conditional mean, to make the point that the line
# is an approximation: m(x) = 2 + 1.4 x - 0.06 x^2 over x in [0, 12].
def m(x):
    return 2 + 1.4 * x - 0.06 * x**2

# Draw a cloud: many outcomes at each of a grid of inputs, with vertical spread.
xs = rng.uniform(0, 12, size=400)
ys = m(xs) + rng.normal(0, 1.6, size=400)

xgrid = np.linspace(0, 12, 200)

# A straight-line approximation fit by eye-equivalent least squares slope/intercept.
b1 = np.cov(xs, ys, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(xs)
b0 = ys.mean() - b1 * xs.mean()

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.0))
ax.scatter(xs, ys, s=10, alpha=0.18, color="0.4", label="outcomes (one cloud)")
ax.plot(xgrid, m(xgrid), color="crimson", lw=2.4,
        label=r"conditional mean $m(x)=E[Y\mid X=x]$")
ax.plot(xgrid, b0 + b1 * xgrid, color="navy", lw=2.0, ls="--",
        label="straight-line model")
ax.set_xlabel("input  $x$")
ax.set_ylabel("outcome  $y$")
ax.legend(loc="lower center", frameon=False, fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Regression estimates the average outcome at each input")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

# "Best line" is meaningless until we define "best"

Now we hit the question that REGR-1 exists to answer, and the reason REGR-0
comes first: **there is no such thing as "the best line" until you have chosen
how to score a line's mistakes.** This sounds pedantic. It is not. Different
scoring rules give *different lines through the same points*, and the choice is
yours to make, not the data's to dictate.

Set up the bookkeeping. A candidate line predicts $\hat y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1
x_i$ for the $i$-th point, whose actual outcome is $y_i$. The mistake on that
point is the **residual**

$$
e_i \;=\; y_i - \hat y_i \;=\; y_i - (\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i),
$$

the vertical gap between the dot and the line. A line is "good" if its residuals
are collectively small — but *collectively small* is exactly the undefined
phrase. We must aggregate the $n$ residuals into a single score, and there are
many ways to do it:

- **Sum of squared residuals**, $\sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2$. Squaring makes every
  error positive (so $+3$ and $-3$ do not cancel) and punishes big misses
  disproportionately — a residual of 4 costs 16, four times the cost of a
  residual of 2. Minimizing this is **least squares**, and it is the choice the
  rest of this track commits to.
- **Sum of absolute residuals**, $\sum_{i=1}^{n} |e_i|$. Also kills the sign,
  but treats a miss of 4 as merely twice as bad as a miss of 2. This is
  *least absolute deviations*; it estimates the conditional *median* rather than
  the mean, and it shrugs off outliers that least squares would chase.
- **Worst single residual**, $\max_i |e_i|$. Minimizing the largest error
  ("minimax") — sensible if one catastrophic miss is unacceptable, common in
  engineering tolerances, almost never used in statistics.

Three rules, three different "best" lines through the *identical* cloud. None is
universally correct; each encodes a different belief about what kind of error
hurts.

```{python}
#| label: fig-loss-choice
#| fig-cap: "Three honest definitions of 'best' give three different lines through the same eight points. Least squares (solid) is pulled toward the high outlier because squaring magnifies large residuals; least absolute deviations (dashed) resists it; minimax (dotted) bends to shrink the single worst gap. 'Best line' is undefined until you pick a column of this menu."
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.optimize import minimize

x = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8], dtype=float)
y = np.array([2.1, 3.9, 5.8, 7.7, 9.6, 11.2, 13.1, 22.0])  # last point an outlier

xg = np.linspace(0, 9, 50)

def fit(loss):
    def obj(p):
        r = y - (p[0] + p[1] * x)
        return loss(r)
    res = minimize(obj, x0=[0.0, 1.0], method="Nelder-Mead")
    return res.x

ls   = fit(lambda r: np.sum(r**2))
lad  = fit(lambda r: np.sum(np.abs(r)))
mmax = fit(lambda r: np.max(np.abs(r)))

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.0))
ax.scatter(x, y, s=40, color="black", zorder=3, label="data")
ax.plot(xg, ls[0]  + ls[1]  * xg, color="navy",    lw=2.0, ls="-",
        label="least squares (mean)")
ax.plot(xg, lad[0] + lad[1] * xg, color="crimson", lw=2.0, ls="--",
        label="least absolute dev. (median)")
ax.plot(xg, mmax[0]+ mmax[1] * xg, color="seagreen",lw=2.0, ls=":",
        label="minimax (worst error)")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$")
ax.set_ylabel("$y$")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Same points, three definitions of “best”")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

So why does *squared* error win the track? Three reasons, each of which we cash
out later:

1. **It targets the mean.** Minimizing squared error estimates $E[Y \mid X]$ —
   the conditional mean we argued for in §1 — whereas absolute error estimates
   the median. If the conditional mean is your target, squared error is the
   matching tool. (REGR-1 shows this with calculus on the sample.)
2. **It has a clean closed-form solution.** Squaring makes the score a smooth,
   bowl-shaped (quadratic) function of $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$; setting its
   derivatives to zero gives two linear equations you can solve by hand. Absolute
   error has a kink at zero and no such tidy formula. (REGR-1 derives the
   formula; REGR-4 reveals it as a geometric projection.)
3. **It is the maximum-likelihood choice under Gaussian noise.** If the scatter
   around the line is normally distributed, least squares is not an arbitrary
   convenience — it is the *most probable* explanation of the data. (REGR-12
   proves this; it is the bridge to the entire GLM branch.)

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Least squares is a *choice*, not a law of nature — and its Achilles' heel is
the outlier.** Because squaring magnifies large residuals, a single wild point
can swing the least-squares line dramatically, as @fig-loss-choice shows. If your
data has heavy tails or contamination, the least-absolute-deviation line (or a
robust method, REGR-11) may be the honest choice. Reaching for least squares
reflexively, without asking what kind of error you are willing to tolerate, is
one of the most common mistakes in applied regression.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"Why do we square the residuals instead of taking absolute values?"** A strong
answer hits all three notes above: squaring (1) targets the conditional *mean*
rather than the median, (2) yields a differentiable, convex objective with a
closed-form solution, and (3) coincides with maximum likelihood under Gaussian
errors. Then add the honest caveat — squared error is *sensitive to outliers*,
which is precisely when you would prefer absolute deviations. Naming both the
upside and the cost is what separates a graduate-level answer from a
memorized one.
:::

# Where this came from: two origin stories

The *why* of regression is easier to feel once you know it was invented twice,
for two different reasons, by people who were not trying to invent it.

## Legendre and Gauss: pinning down the heavens (least squares)

The method came first; the name came later. Around the turn of the nineteenth
century, the hardest data problem in the world was astronomical and geodetic:
given many imperfect measurements — of a comet's position night after night, or
of the length of a degree of arc along a meridian to determine the figure of the
Earth — find the orbit, or the shape, that best reconciles them. The
measurements *disagreed*, because every instrument and observer carried error,
and there were more equations than unknowns. What does "best fit" mean when no
single curve can pass through every observation?

**Adrien-Marie Legendre** published the answer in 1805, in an appendix on comet
orbits, and stated the principle with striking clarity: choose the values that
make the **sum of the squares of the errors** as small as possible. He called it
*la méthode des moindres carrés* — the method of least squares — and gave the
modern reason for squaring: it prevents positive and negative errors from
cancelling, and it lets large errors weigh more, so the fit is dragged toward
consensus.

**Carl Friedrich Gauss** claimed — credibly, and to Legendre's lasting
irritation — to have been using the method since around 1795, and in 1809 he
supplied what Legendre had not: a *probabilistic justification*. Gauss showed
that *if* the observation errors follow what we now call the normal (Gaussian)
distribution, then least squares yields the most probable values of the
unknowns. This is the maximum-likelihood argument in embryo — the exact result
REGR-12 derives in full — and it is why the bell curve and least squares have
been welded together ever since. Gauss's public triumph was concrete: in 1801
the dwarf planet **Ceres** was discovered, tracked briefly, then lost behind the
Sun. From a sparse handful of observations Gauss predicted where it would
re-emerge, and astronomers found it almost exactly where his least-squares
computation said it would be. The method that lets us draw a line through a
scatterplot was forged to find a lost world in the sky.

The *why* to carry forward: least squares was born to **reconcile conflicting
measurements of a quantity you cannot observe directly** — to extract signal
from a pile of noisy, over-determined data. That is still exactly what
regression does. Every time you fit a line, you are doing Gauss's Ceres problem
in miniature.

## Galton: regression to the mean (and the word itself)

The *word* "regression" comes from a different problem entirely, and from a
discovery so counterintuitive it still trips people up. In the 1880s the
Victorian polymath **Francis Galton** was studying heredity — how a trait passes
from parents to offspring. He gathered data on the heights of parents and their
adult children (and, in a cleaner parallel experiment, on the diameters of
sweet-pea seeds across generations), plotted children's heights against parents'
heights, and found something he did not expect.

Tall parents did tend to have tall children — but **on average, the children
were closer to the population mean than their parents were.** The sons of very
tall fathers were tall, yet typically *shorter* than their fathers; the sons of
very short fathers were short, yet typically *taller* than their fathers. The
extremes pulled *inward* toward the average from one generation to the next.
Galton first called this "reversion," then "regression toward mediocrity" — and
that word, *regression*, attached itself to the line he drew through the cloud
and never let go. The slope he found was less than 1: a father one inch above
average had sons on average only about two-thirds of an inch above average.

```{python}
#| label: fig-galton
#| fig-cap: "Galton's regression to the mean, simulated transparently. Fathers' and sons' heights are generated with equal spread and correlation 0.5, so neither generation is shrinking. Yet the regression line (red, slope $\\approx 0.5$) is flatter than the dashed 45-degree 'sons equal fathers' line. Read off the tallest fathers: their sons average shorter than they are; the shortest fathers' sons average taller. The extremes regress toward the mean."
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 600
mu, sd, rho = 69.0, 2.7, 0.5  # inches; equal spread; correlation 0.5

# Bivariate normal with equal marginals and correlation rho -> regression slope = rho.
father = rng.normal(mu, sd, n)
son = mu + rho * (father - mu) + rng.normal(0, sd * np.sqrt(1 - rho**2), n)

slope = np.cov(father, son, bias=True)[0, 1] / np.var(father)
intercept = son.mean() - slope * father.mean()
xg = np.linspace(father.min(), father.max(), 50)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.4, 4.2))
ax.scatter(father, son, s=12, alpha=0.30, color="0.45")
ax.plot(xg, xg, color="0.2", ls="--", lw=1.6, label="sons = fathers (slope 1)")
ax.plot(xg, intercept + slope * xg, color="crimson", lw=2.4,
        label=f"regression line (slope $\\approx${slope:.2f})")
ax.axvline(mu, color="0.7", lw=0.8)
ax.axhline(mu, color="0.7", lw=0.8)
ax.set_xlabel("father's height (inches)")
ax.set_ylabel("son's height (inches)")
ax.legend(loc="upper left", frameon=False, fontsize=8)
ax.set_title("Regression to the mean")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

Here is the part everyone gets wrong, and the part worth demystifying once and
for all: **regression to the mean is not a force, not heredity weakening, not
populations collapsing toward average height.** In @fig-galton the two
generations have *exactly the same spread* — nothing is shrinking. The effect is
a pure consequence of **imperfect correlation**. Whenever two variables are
correlated by less than 1, an extreme value of one is, on average, paired with a
*less* extreme value of the other, simply because an extreme observation is
partly skill (or genuine tallness) and partly luck, and luck does not repeat. A
father who is unusually tall is tall partly for heritable reasons that pass on
and partly for a roll of the dice that does not. Average over the dice and the
son slides toward the middle.

This is why the regression line is *flatter* than the 45-degree line — and the
slope of a standardized regression line is exactly the correlation coefficient,
a fact REGR-1 makes precise. Galton handed us not just a word but a permanent
warning, which appears all over applied science:

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Regression to the mean masquerades as a real effect.** Take the worst-scoring
students, give them a tutoring program, retest them: they improve — *even if the
program does nothing*, because their first scores were partly bad luck that
won't repeat. Praise a pilot after a great landing and the next is worse; scold
after a bad one and the next is better — not because scolding works, but because
extremes regress. Any time you select on an extreme and then measure again,
expect movement toward the mean *for free*. Mistaking that statistical
inevitability for a causal effect is one of the most expensive errors in
medicine, education, sports, and business analytics.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
**"Explain regression to the mean to someone non-technical, and say why it
matters."** The grad-level answer: it is what happens when you select cases on an
extreme value of one variable and then look at a second, imperfectly correlated
variable — the second tends to be less extreme, *purely* because the
correlation is below 1, with no causal mechanism required. The reason it matters
is that it manufactures fake treatment effects: pick the sickest patients, the
worst-performing stores, the coldest months, do *anything*, and you will see
improvement that a naive analysis credits to your intervention. The defense is a
control group. Connecting the phenomenon to "correlation $< 1$" and to its
practical danger is the answer interviewers are listening for.
:::

# When is "fit a line" the right move — and when not?

We promised an honest applicability call, and this is where REGR-0 earns its
place as the opener: before you spend the rest of the track learning *how* to
fit lines, you should know *when* to.

**Reach for a straight-line regression when:**

- The relationship between $X$ and the *average* of $Y$ is plausibly
  monotonic and roughly straight over the range you care about — or you only need
  a local, first-order approximation.
- You want an **interpretable** summary: a slope you can report, defend, and
  attach uncertainty to. This is the explanation question, and the line is
  almost unmatched for it.
- Data is **limited** relative to the complexity of the truth. A line's rigidity
  protects you from overfitting noise; a flexible curve with few points will
  betray you on new data.

**Be suspicious, and reach for something else, when:**

- The conditional mean is genuinely **curved or has a threshold** — diminishing
  returns, saturation, a tipping point. A line will systematically over- and
  under-predict in stripes (you will *see* it in the residuals, REGR-10). Fix:
  transform a variable, add a polynomial or spline term (REGR-20, REGR-21), or
  go nonparametric (REGR-23, REGR-24).
- The outcome is **not a free real number** — a yes/no, a count, a probability.
  Fitting an ordinary line to a 0/1 outcome can predict probabilities below 0 or
  above 1, which is nonsense. Fix: a generalized linear model — logistic for
  binary (REGR-17), Poisson for counts (REGR-19).
- The data has **heavy-tailed noise or outliers**. Least squares will chase
  them. Fix: robust or absolute-deviation methods (REGR-11).
- You actually want a **causal** slope, not just an associational one. Regression
  gives you "outcomes tend to be higher when the input is higher"; turning that
  into "raising the input *causes* higher outcomes" requires assumptions
  (controlled experiments, or careful causal-inference machinery) that the line
  itself does not supply. Galton's warning is the tip of this iceberg.

The honest summary: the line is a **deliberate, interpretable approximation to
the conditional mean**, superb when the mean is roughly straight and the noise is
well-behaved, and a trap when it is not. The entire Regression track is, in a
sense, the systematic story of *detecting* when the line is too simple and
*earning* the right to make it more flexible — without ever losing the
interpretability that made it worth drawing in the first place.

# Connections — where the track goes from here

This article posed the question; the rest of the track answers it.

- **REGR-1 — OLS, One Predictor.** Commits to squared-error loss and *derives*
  the slope $\hat\beta_1 = S_{xy}/S_{xx}$ and intercept $\hat\beta_0 = \bar y -
  \hat\beta_1 \bar x$ with full Calc-1 calculus. This is the true computational
  entry point; everything in §3's "least squares wins" claim is cashed out there.
- **REGR-2 — What the Line Assumes.** States precisely the conditions under
  which the least-squares line is a *good* estimator of $E[Y \mid X]$ — making
  rigorous the hand-waving about "noise" and "average" we used here.
- **REGR-12 — The Maximum-Likelihood View.** Proves Gauss's claim: under
  Gaussian errors, least squares *is* maximum likelihood — the §3 reason (3) and
  the doorway to the GLM branch.
- **REGR-23 — Kernel Regression.** Returns to estimate $E[Y \mid X]$ *directly*,
  as a local weighted average, with no line at all — the purest expression of
  the Core, and the answer to "what if the mean really is curved?"

# Going deeper

- **Legendre, A.-M. (1805).** *Nouvelles méthodes pour la détermination des
  orbites des comètes.* The appendix that first published least squares.
- **Gauss, C. F. (1809).** *Theoria Motus Corporum Coelestium.* The
  probabilistic justification and the Ceres computation; the origin of the
  least-squares–Gaussian link.
- **Galton, F. (1886).** "Regression towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature,"
  *Journal of the Anthropological Institute.* The paper that named the field and
  first charted regression to the mean.
- **Stigler, S. M. (1986).** *The History of Statistics: The Measurement of
  Uncertainty before 1900.* The definitive account of the Legendre–Gauss
  priority dispute and Galton's path to regression.
- **Kahneman, D. (2011).** *Thinking, Fast and Slow*, ch. 17. A modern,
  accessible treatment of regression to the mean as a cognitive trap — the
  flight-instructor anecdote in §4 is his.
